perturbation.original_text: Constellation will provide, or cause to be provided, all of the Services included in the Products & Services in compliance in all material respects with all applicable laws, rules, regulations, codes, orders, treaties and other requirements of federal/national, state/provincial and local governments and agencies thereof, including but not limited those relating to labor, health, safety and the environment.
perturbation.changed_text: Constellation will endeavor to provide all of the Services included in the Products & Services, aiming for compliance with all applicable laws, rules, regulations, codes, orders, treaties and other requirements of federal/national, state/provincial and local governments and agencies thereof, including but not limited those relating to labor, health, safety and the environment.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'in compliance in all material respects' to 'aiming for compliance' introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of adherence to applicable laws. 'Endeavor to provide' further weakens the obligation, making it less definite.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 4101.11 mandates employers to furnish a place of employment free from recognized hazards causing or likely to cause death or serious physical harm to employees. By changing 'in compliance' to 'aiming for compliance,' the modified text suggests a non-mandatory approach to workplace safety, potentially contradicting the legal requirement for a safe working environment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 4101.11 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 4101.11 | Duty of employer to protect employees and frequenters. Ohio Revised Code / Title 41 Labor and Industry / Chapter 4101 Safety In The Workplace Effective: October 1, 1953 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Every employer shall furnish employment which is safe for the employees engaged therein, shall furnish a place of employment which shall be safe for the employees therein and for frequenters thereof, shall furnish and use safety devices and safeguards, shall adopt and use methods and processes, follow and obey orders, and prescribe hours of labor reasonably adequate to render such employment and places of employment safe, and shall do every other thing reasonably necessary to protect the life, health, safety, and welfare of such employees and frequenters. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1953 – House Bill 1 - 100th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1953 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the original requirement of 'in compliance in all material respects' with 'aiming for compliance,' which significantly weakens the obligation to adhere to laws. Ohio Revised Code § 4101.11, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates that every employer 'shall furnish employment which is safe for the employees engaged therein, shall furnish a place of employment which shall be safe for the employees therein.' By only 'aiming for compliance,' the revised contract language creates a contradiction because it no longer guarantees the legally required safe working environment, potentially leading to non-compliance. The original text ensured adherence to safety regulations, while the modified version only expresses an intention, making the obligation ambiguous and less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Constellation shall, at its own expense, secure and maintain in full force and effect throughout the Term (a) insurance coverage for defamation, trademark and service mark infringement, unfair competition, copyright infringement, and infringement of a person's right of publicity and right of privacy from a carrier with an A.M. Best rating of A10 or better in an amount not less than [***] per occurrence; and (b) a general liability insurance policy from a carrier with an A.M. Best rating of A10 or better in an amount not less than [***] in aggregate.
perturbation.changed_text: Constellation shall try to secure and maintain insurance coverage throughout the Term (a) insurance coverage for defamation, trademark and service mark infringement, unfair competition, copyright infringement, and infringement of a person's right of publicity and right of privacy from what they perceive to be a reputable carrier in an amount they consider reasonable per occurrence; and (b) a general liability insurance policy from what they perceive to be a reputable carrier in an amount they consider reasonable in aggregate.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall... secure and maintain' with 'shall try to secure and maintain' introduces uncertainty about the obligation. Replacing 'A.M. Best rating of A10 or better' and 'not less than [***]' with 'what they perceive to be a reputable carrier' and 'they consider reasonable' adds significant ambiguity, making the insurance requirement subjective and difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 3937.18 dictates requirements for uninsured motorist coverage and mandates specific standards for insurance policies. The modified text allows Constellation to determine what is 'reputable' and 'reasonable' for insurance coverage. This conflicts with the state's mandated minimums and objective standards for insurance carrier ratings and coverage amounts, thus failing to ensure adequate protection as required by law. The second link serves as a backup, as the statute is behind a paywall.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 3937.18 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 3937.18 | Uninsured and underinsured motorist coverage. Ohio Revised Code / Title 39 Insurance / Chapter 3937 Casualty Insurance; Motor Vehicle Insurance Effective: March 14, 2023 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 56 - 134th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Any policy of insurance delivered or issued for delivery in this state with respect to any motor vehicle registered or principally garaged in this state that insures against loss resulting from liability imposed by law for bodily injury or death suffered by any person arising out of the ownership, maintenance, or use of a motor vehicle, may, but is not required to, include uninsured motorist coverage, underinsured motorist coverage, or both uninsured and underinsured motorist coverages. Unless otherwise defined in the policy or any endorsement to the policy, "motor vehicle," for purposes of the uninsured motorist coverage, underinsured motorist coverage, or both uninsured and underinsured motorist coverages, means a self-propelled vehicle designed for use and principally used on public roads, including an automobile, truck, semi-tractor, motorcycle, and bus. "Motor vehicle" also includes a motor home, provided the motor home is not stationary and is not being used as a temporary or permanent residence or office. "Motor vehicle" does not include a trolley, streetcar, trailer, railroad engine, railroad car, motorized bicycle, golf cart, off-road recreational vehicle, snowmobile, fork lift, aircraft, watercraft, construction equipment, farm tractor or other vehicle designed and principally used for agricultural purposes, mobile home, vehicle traveling on treads or rails, or any simil
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific insurance coverage ('secure and maintain') with defined standards ('A.M. Best rating of A10 or better' and a specific amount). The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating 'shall try to secure' and replacing objective standards with subjective ones ('reputable carrier' and 'reasonable' amount). This contradicts the intention of Ohio Revised Code § 3937.18, as the changed text allows Constellation to determine what is 'reputable' and 'reasonable,' conflicting with the state's minimums and objective standards. The snippet indicates the law pertains to insurance policies in Ohio relating to motor vehicles and liability imposed by law. While the snippet does not explicitly state 'minimums and objective standards,' the shift to subjective criteria creates a loophole where Constellation might not secure adequate coverage, potentially undermining the protections the law intends to provide against liability.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Constellation fails to pay to the HOF Entities when due any sum required by this Agreement to be paid, whether pursuant to this Section 3.4 or otherwise, interest shall accrue from the date due on the unpaid amount at the rate of [***] per month or, if less, the highest rate permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Constellation fails to pay to the HOF Entities any sum, the parties will try to negotiate an appropriate interest rate, if any, on the unpaid amount.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that interest *shall* accrue at a defined rate if payments are not made. The modified text replaces this with a vague commitment to *try* to negotiate an *appropriate* interest rate, eliminating the mandatory nature of interest accrual and making the rate discretionary. This is an ambiguous legal obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01 sets the legal rate of interest when the parties do not agree upon a different rate. By making the interest rate negotiable and discretionary, the modified text contradicts the state law that provides a default interest rate in the absence of a specific agreement. While parties can agree to a different rate, eliminating a guaranteed rate altogether contravenes the spirit of the law, which seeks to ensure creditors receive at least a minimum level of compensation for delayed payments. The second URL provides a backup source in case the first is unavailable, summarizing the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1343.01 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1343.01 | Maximum rate of interest. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1343 Interest Effective: June 29, 1988 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 130 - 117th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) The parties to a bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing for the forbearance or payment of money at any future time, may stipulate therein for the payment of interest upon the amount thereof at any rate not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually, except as authorized in division (B) of this section. (B) Any party may agree to pay a rate of interest in excess of the maximum rate provided in division (A) of this section when: (1) The original amount of the principal indebtedness stipulated in the bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing exceeds one hundred thousand dollars; (2) The payment is to a broker or dealer registered under the "Securities Exchange Act of 1934," 48 Stat. 881, 15 U.S.C. 78A, as amended, for carrying a debit balance in an account for a customer if such debit balance is payable on demand and secured by stocks, bonds or other securities; (3) The instrument evidences a loan secured by a mortgage or deed of trust on real estate where the loan has been approved, insured, guaranteed, purchased, or for which an offer or commitment to insure, guarantee, or purchase has been received, in whole or in part, by the federal government or any agency or instrumentality thereof, the federal national mortgage association, the federal home loan mortgage corporation, or the farmers home administration, all of which is authorized pursuant to the "National Housing Act,"

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01. The original text mandates interest accrual on unpaid amounts, while the modified text replaces this with a negotiation for an "appropriate" interest rate. The Law Explanation states that the law sets a legal rate of interest when the parties do not agree upon a different rate. The changed text makes the interest rate discretionary, which goes against the spirit of the law that seeks to ensure creditors receive at least a minimum level of compensation for delayed payments, as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "The parties to a bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing for the forbearance or payment of money at any future time, may stipulate therein for the payment of interest upon the amount thereof at any rate not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually". This Snippet suggests that while parties can stipulate an interest rate, the modified text eliminates a guaranteed rate altogether.
